section.title: The Levite and His Concubine
section.sectionRationale: The second epilogue of Judges opens with the darkest narrative in the book. A Levite, a man who should embody Israel's holiness, takes a concubine, retrieves her from her father's house after a long delay, and on the road home finds no welcome in an Israelite town. At Gibeah of Benjamin an old sojourner takes them in, but that night the men of the city surround the house, and a horror echoing Sodom unfolds: the concubine is thrust out, gang raped through the night, and left dead at the door. The Levite carries her home, cuts her body into twelve pieces, and sends them through Israel with a summons to reckon with what has happened.
section.thesis: When there is no king and every man does as he pleases, even the covenant people can sink to the depravity of Sodom; the narrator lays the atrocity bare, without excuse, so that Israel, and we, will cry out for a righteous King who protects the vulnerable and judges evil.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the Levite retrieves his concubine amid endless delay, verses 1 to 10; the fateful turn to Gibeah rather than pagan Jebus, verses 11 to 21; the atrocity at Gibeah, verses 22 to 26; and the dismembered body and the shocking summons to all Israel, verses 27 to 30.

verses.text: Now in those days, when there was no {{melek|king}} in Israel, a Levite who lived in the remote hill country of Ephraim took for himself a {{pilegesh|concubine}} from Bethlehem in Judah.
But she was unfaithful to him and left him to return to her father’s house in Bethlehem in Judah. After she had been there four months,

her husband got up and went after her to speak kindly to her and bring her back, taking his servant and a pair of donkeys. So the girl brought him into her father’s house, and when her father saw him, he gladly welcomed him.
His father-in-law, the girl’s father, persuaded him to stay, so he remained with him three days, eating, drinking, and lodging there.
On the fourth day, they got up early in the morning and prepared to depart, but the girl’s father said to his son-in-law, “Refresh your heart with a morsel of bread, and then you can go.”
So they sat down and the two of them ate and drank together. Then the girl’s father said to the man, “Please agree to stay overnight and let your heart be merry.”
The man got up to depart, but his father-in-law persuaded him, so he stayed there that night.
On the fifth day, he got up early in the morning to depart, but the girl’s father said, “Please refresh your heart.” So they waited until late afternoon and the two of them ate.

When the man got up to depart with his concubine and his servant, his father-in-law, the girl’s father, said to him, “Look, the day is drawing to a close. Please spend the night. See, the day is almost over. Spend the night here, that your heart may be merry. Then you can get up early tomorrow for your journey home.”
But the man was unwilling to spend the night. He got up and departed, and arrived opposite Jebus (that is, Jerusalem), with his two saddled donkeys and his concubine.

units.label: The fateful turn to Gibeah
units.note: no one would take them in

verses.text: When they were near Jebus and the day was almost gone, the servant said to his master, “Please, let us stop at this Jebusite city and spend the night here.”
But his master replied, “We will not turn aside to the city of foreigners, where there are no Israelites. We will go on to Gibeah.”
He continued, “Come, let us try to reach one of these towns to spend the night in Gibeah or Ramah.”
So they continued on their journey, and the sun set as they neared Gibeah in Benjamin.
They stopped to go in and lodge in Gibeah. The Levite went in and sat down in the city square, but no one would take them into his home for the night.
That evening an old man from the hill country of Ephraim, who was residing in Gibeah (the men of that place were Benjamites), came in from his work in the field.
When he looked up and saw the traveler in the city square, the old man asked, “Where are you going, and where have you come from?”

The Levite replied, “We are traveling from Bethlehem in Judah to the remote hill country of Ephraim, where I am from. I went to Bethlehem in Judah, and now I am going to the house of the LORD; but no one has taken me into his home,
even though there is both straw and feed for our donkeys, and bread and wine for me and the maidservant and young man with me. There is nothing that we, your servants, lack.”
“Peace to you,” said the old man. “Let me supply everything you need. Only do not spend the night in the square.”
So he brought him to his house and fed his donkeys. And they washed their feet and ate and drank.

While they were enjoying themselves, suddenly the wicked men of the city surrounded the house. Pounding on the door, they said to the old man who owned the house, “Bring out the man who came to your house, so we can have relations with him!”
The owner of the house went out and said to them, “No, my brothers, do not do this wicked thing! After all, this man is a guest in my house. Do not commit this {{nebalah|outrage}}.
Look, let me bring out my virgin daughter and the man’s concubine, and you can use them and do with them as you wish. But do not do such a vile thing to this man.”
But the men would not listen to him. So the Levite took his concubine and sent her outside to them, and they raped her and abused her throughout the night, and at dawn they let her go.
Early that morning, the woman went back to the house where her master was staying, collapsed at the doorway, and lay there until it was light.

units.label: The dismembered body and the summons
units.note: think it over, take counsel, and speak up

verses.text: In the morning, when her master got up and opened the doors of the house to go out on his journey, there was his concubine, collapsed in the doorway of the house, with her hands on the threshold.
“Get up,” he told her. “Let us go.” But there was no response. So the man put her on his donkey and set out for home.
When he reached his house, he picked up a knife, took hold of his concubine, cut her limb by limb into twelve pieces, and sent her throughout the territory of Israel.
And everyone who saw it said, “Nothing like this has been seen or done from the day the Israelites came out of the land of Egypt until this day. Think it over, take counsel, and speak up!”

melek.gloss: King. The refrain of the epilogues, there was no king in Israel, frames chapters 19 to 21. It names the vacuum in which this horror unfolds: no righteous authority to restrain evil or defend the weak. The book's final line, 21:25, repeats it, so the whole nightmare becomes an argument for the true and righteous King who is coming.

pilegesh.gloss: Concubine, a secondary wife of lower legal status. The word marks the woman's vulnerability from the first verse: she has a place in the household yet little protection. The narrative exposes how she is used, handed over, abandoned, and finally sacrificed by the very men bound to shield her.

nebalah.gloss: Outrage, disgraceful folly, wanton wickedness that shatters the covenant order. It is the strongest Hebrew word for a moral atrocity, used of Sodom-like sin. The old man begs the mob not to commit this nebalah, and Israel will call the whole affair a nebalah, 20:6; the narrator wants us to feel the full weight of it as evil, not mere misfortune.

basic.context: This is the darkest chapter in Judges, and the narrator tells it with the repeated note that there was no king in Israel, so everyone did as he pleased. A Levite, a man set apart for God, takes a concubine, then travels to bring her back from her father's house after months of estrangement. Delayed again and again by her father's hospitality, they leave late and, refusing to lodge in a pagan city, turn instead to Gibeah in Benjamin, expecting safety among God's people. But no Israelite takes them in; only an old outsider does. That night the men of the town, echoing the men of Sodom, surround the house demanding to abuse the guest. In a scene of cowardice and horror the concubine is thrust out and raped to death, and the Levite carries her body home, cuts it into twelve pieces, and sends them through Israel to force the nation to reckon with the evil.
basic.hermeneutics: This account describes a horror; it does not commend a single act in it. The Levite, the mob, the old man's dreadful offer of his daughter, the dismemberment, all stand under the narrator's silent judgment and the frame, there was no king. Scripture tells the truth about the depths of human sin, even among the covenant people, without softening it. We must read it as a text of terror that indicts, not a model to imitate, and let it drive us to long for the righteous King.
basic.originalLanguages: The frame word is melek, king: there was no king in Israel. The victim is a pilegesh, a concubine, whose lower status hints at her exposure. Twice the sin is named nebalah, outrage, the word for Sodom-like wickedness that tears the fabric of the covenant. The vocabulary refuses to let us treat the atrocity as anything less than grievous evil.
basic.typology: The chapter is a dark mirror. Gibeah becomes a new Sodom inside Israel, and the innocent one is sacrificed by those meant to protect her, exposing the desperate need for a deliverer. Against this backdrop the true and better Bridegroom stands out: where the Levite handed over his bride to death to save himself, Christ gave Himself up to death to save His bride.
basic.christ: There is no hero in this chapter; that is the point. The absence of a righteous king who defends the defenceless cries out for the King who will come. And the broken body of an innocent victim, sent out to summon Israel to reckon with sin, is answered at last by another broken body, the body of Christ, given not by a coward to save himself but by the Lord Himself to save the guilty. Where Gibeah shows what people do to the vulnerable, the cross shows what God does for them.
basic.god: God seems silent in this chapter, and that silence is part of its horror; He is present as the offended holy One whose covenant is being trampled. Yet the very telling of the story is His verdict: this is nebalah, outrage. The God who appears absent here is the God who hears the cry of the abused, who will not leave evil unjudged, and who is moving history toward the King and the cross where sin is finally answered.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 19 tells of Sodom, the pattern Gibeah repeats. Hosea 9 and 10 recall the days of Gibeah as Israel's depth of corruption. Judges 21:25 gives the closing verdict, everyone did what was right in his own eyes. And Ephesians 5 shows Christ, the Bridegroom who gives Himself for His bride, the opposite of the Levite.
basic.oneStory: This atrocity is the floor of the book of Judges, the proof of how far a people fall when everyone does what is right in his own eyes. Its unrelieved darkness is meant to make us ache for a righteous King, and it points ahead to Christ, the true King and true Bridegroom, who does not sacrifice the innocent to save himself but lays down his own life to rescue the guilty.

context.literary: The first of two appendices, chapters 19 to 21, that close Judges without a named judge. It is deliberately shaped to echo Genesis 19: strangers in a city square, an old host, a mob at the door demanding the male guest, women offered instead. The parallels indict Israel by showing that Gibeah has become Sodom. The endless hospitality of the opening scene, warm and almost comic, makes the sudden horror of the night all the more shocking.
context.historical: Concubinage and the honor bound to hospitality were real features of the ancient world; a guest under one's roof was to be protected at all costs, which is why the old man is horrified. Gibeah was a town of Benjamin, later the home of King Saul, which sharpens the tragedy: the tribe that will give Israel its first king is here steeped in Sodom's sin. The Levite's summons by dismembered body parts parallels a later summons by Saul in 1 Samuel 11, tying the epilogue to the monarchy that follows.
context.canonical: Judges 19 marks the nadir of the period of the judges and sets up the civil war and near-extinction of Benjamin in chapters 20 to 21. The prophets remember it: Hosea twice points back to the days of Gibeah as the low-water mark of Israel's sin. In the larger canon it stands as a stark witness to human depravity apart from God's righteous rule, and thus as part of the long preparation for the King, David and finally Christ, who will reign in justice.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To expose the moral collapse of Israel under the rule of self, to indict Gibeah as a new Sodom, and to make the reader long for the righteous king the book keeps saying is absent. The dismembered body and the closing summons force the nation, and the reader, to stop and reckon with the evil.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Everything here is description, not prescription. The narrator reports the Levite's cowardice, the old man's terrible offer, the mob's violence, and the dismemberment without endorsing any of it. The abiding truths are the reality and depth of human sin, the preciousness of the victim in God's sight, and the desperate need for a righteous King; nothing in the chapter is a pattern to follow.

debates.question: How should we read such a text of terror, and why is it in Scripture?

views.label: An honest indictment meant to horrify
views.case: The narrative deliberately withholds comment so that the horror speaks for itself; by refusing to look away, Scripture tells the truth about what sin does, honors the victim by naming the evil done to her, and drives the reader to grief, repentance, and longing for a righteous King.

views.label: A structural argument for the monarchy and, ultimately, for Christ
views.case: The frame there was no king ties the atrocity to the book's thesis: without righteous rule, Israel devours the weak; the text is placed here to prepare for the coming of a king who will do justice, a hope only fully met in Christ.

debates.bearing: Both readings guard us from sensationalism: we read soberly, we let the evil be named as evil, and we let the darkness push us toward the King who protects the vulnerable and judges sin.

debates.question: How culpable is the Levite?

views.label: Deeply culpable
views.case: He thrusts his own concubine out to the mob to save himself, shows no tenderness at dawn, gives a self-serving account later in chapter 20, and treats her body as a tool for his summons; the narrative quietly exposes him as a coward, not a victim.

views.label: One sinner among many
views.case: The chapter spreads guilt widely, the mob, the town, the culture that offers women in a man's place, so that the Levite's failure is part of a whole society's collapse rather than the sin of a single villain; the point is systemic corruption under self-rule.

debates.bearing: Either way the man meant to represent Israel's holiness is complicit, which underlines that the rot reaches even the religious leadership and that no one in the story is the deliverer Israel needs.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter is bracketed by melek, king: in those days there was no king in Israel. That absence is the soil in which the horror grows, for with no righteous authority every man does as he pleases and the weak are devoured. The victim is a pilegesh, a concubine, a wife of lower standing whose very title signals how exposed she is; she is spoken for, handed over, and never once allowed to speak. Twice the evil is named nebalah, outrage, the strongest word for wanton, covenant-shattering wickedness, the same category as the sin of Sodom. The old man pleads, do not commit this nebalah, and Israel will later call it a nebalah committed in Israel. The words will not let us minimize what happened: this is not misfortune but atrocity, and its telling is itself an accusation that cries out for a King who will do right.

typology.type: Gibeah as a new Sodom within Israel
typology.fulfillment: The exposure of sin so deep that only a Saviour will do; where judgment fell on Sodom, God in Christ bears judgment to rescue sinners, Genesis 19 and Romans 5:8.

typology.type: The absent righteous king who should defend the weak
typology.fulfillment: Christ the King who executes justice for the oppressed and shelters the vulnerable, Psalm 72 and Isaiah 9:6-7.

typology.type: The broken body sent out to summon Israel to reckon with sin
typology.fulfillment: The broken body of Christ, given willingly, that gathers a people and confronts the world with its sin and God's mercy, 1 Corinthians 11:24 and John 12:32.

study.christ: This chapter offers no hero, and its very hopelessness preaches Christ. The refrain there was no king in Israel exposes the vacuum that only the righteous King can fill; where Gibeah shows a society that devours the defenceless, Jesus is the King who came to seek and save the lost and to bring justice to the oppressed, Isaiah 42:1-4. The Levite handed over his bride to death to spare himself; Christ, the true Bridegroom, handed Himself over to death to save His bride, Ephesians 5:25. And the innocent, broken body sent through Israel to force a reckoning with sin is answered by the willingly broken body of the Lord, given for us, that both exposes our evil and covers it. In the darkest chapter of Judges, the light is what is missing, and the whole book leans forward to the King who would come.
study.god: God does not speak in Judges 19, and that apparent silence is part of the terror; yet He is not absent. He is the holy One whose covenant is being torn to pieces, the God to whose house the Levite claims to be going even as he behaves faithlessly. The narrator's refusal to excuse anything is God's own verdict rendered through the telling: this is nebalah, outrage. The God who seems hidden here is the God who hears the cry of the abused, who remembers the victim the world forgot, and who will not let evil go unanswered. His justice will fall, and His mercy will come, in the King and the cross toward which the whole story strains.

crossReferences.note: The men of Sodom surround Lot's house demanding the male guests; Gibeah repeats the pattern, indicting Israel as a new Sodom.
They have deeply corrupted themselves as in the days of Gibeah; the prophet remembers this atrocity as the depth of Israel's sin.
There was no king in Israel; everyone did what was right in his own eyes, the closing verdict that frames this whole horror.
Christ loved the church and gave Himself up for her, the true Bridegroom who is everything the Levite was not.

study.oneStory: Judges 19 is the floor of the book, the proof of how far a covenant people fall when there is no king and every man does what is right in his own eyes. Its unbroken darkness, a concubine offered up and destroyed, a Levite who saves himself, a town gone the way of Sodom, is meant to make us ache. And that ache is the point: the whole story is bending toward a righteous King who protects the weak and judges evil, and toward Christ, the true King and true Bridegroom, who lays down His own life rather than sacrifice the innocent, and who bears our judgment to make us His own.

quiz.q: What refrain frames this chapter and the whole second epilogue of Judges?

options.text: In those days there was no king in Israel, so everyone did as he pleased.
options.note: The absence of a righteous king is the frame that interprets the whole atrocity.

options.text: The people cried out and the LORD raised up a deliverer.
options.note: That is the cycle earlier in Judges, but the epilogue's frame is the lack of a king.

options.text: The land had rest for forty years.
options.note: A refrain from earlier chapters, not the frame of this dark epilogue.

options.text: The LORD was with Judah.
options.note: This does not frame chapter 19; the theme is that there was no king.

quiz.q: Why did the Levite refuse to lodge in Jebus and press on to Gibeah?

options.text: Because Jebus was a city of foreigners and he expected safety among fellow Israelites in Gibeah.
options.note: Verse 12 shows his reasoning, which the story tragically overturns.

options.text: Because Gibeah was closer and it was already dark.
options.note: Distance and daylight were a factor, but his stated reason was avoiding a pagan town for an Israelite one.

options.text: Because he had relatives waiting for him in Gibeah.
options.note: No relatives are mentioned; he expected general hospitality among God's people.

options.text: Because the LORD had told him to go to Gibeah.
options.note: There is no divine direction here; it was his own choice, and it ended in horror.

quiz.q: How does the scene at Gibeah deliberately echo an earlier passage of Scripture?

options.text: It mirrors Sodom in Genesis 19: a mob surrounds the house demanding the male guest, and women are offered in his place.
options.note: The parallels indict Gibeah as a new Sodom within Israel.

options.text: It mirrors the plagues on Egypt.
options.note: There is judgment language in Judges, but the direct echo here is Sodom, not the plagues.

options.text: It mirrors Abraham welcoming the three visitors.
options.note: There is hospitality imagery, but the pointed echo is Sodom's wickedness, not Abraham's welcome.

options.text: It has no connection to any earlier passage.
options.note: The Sodom parallels are unmistakable and intentional.

quiz.q: How should we understand the terrible events of this chapter?

options.text: As description that indicts the evil, not prescription; the narrator condemns it by the way he tells it and by the frame there was no king.
options.note: Scripture reports the horror to expose sin, not to approve any act in it.

options.text: As a sad story with no clear moral point.
options.note: It is sober and terrible, but it has a clear purpose: to expose sin and long for the King.

options.text: As an example of how disputes should be settled.
options.note: Nothing in the chapter is a model to follow; it is a text of terror.

options.text: As God's approval of the Levite's actions.
options.note: The narrative silently condemns the Levite; God approves nothing here.

quiz.q: How does the chapter's unrelieved darkness point us toward Christ?

options.text: The absence of a righteous king who defends the weak makes us long for the true King and Bridegroom, who lays down His own life to save others.
options.note: The vacuum in the story is filled only by Christ, the opposite of the self-saving Levite.

options.text: It shows that people can save themselves if they try hard enough.
options.note: The opposite: no one in the story can save, which is why we need Christ.

options.text: It teaches that God does not care about the suffering of the vulnerable.
options.note: The telling itself is God's verdict against the evil; He remembers the victim.

options.text: It has nothing to do with Christ at all.
options.note: The longing for a righteous King runs straight to Christ.

questions: The chapter keeps repeating that there was no king, so everyone did what was right in his own eyes. Where do you see the fruit of self-rule, doing what seems right to us apart from God, in our own culture and in your own heart?

The Levite handed over the vulnerable to save himself. Where are you tempted to protect yourself at the expense of those you are responsible to shield, and how does Christ's opposite example confront you?
Scripture does not look away from this horror or excuse it. How does it comfort you that God tells the truth about evil, names it as outrage, and remembers the victim the world forgot?
This is a text of terror with no hero in it. How does the very absence of a rescuer here deepen your longing for Christ, the righteous King who defends the weak and judges sin?
The broken body was sent out to force Israel to reckon with sin. How does the willingly broken body of Christ both expose your sin and cover it, and how should that shape your worship this week?

translationNotes.note: The BSB reads she was unfaithful to him; a footnote records that the Septuagint reads she became angry with him. Either way she leaves and returns to her father's house, and the Levite comes to bring her back.

The BSB has the Levite say he is going to the house of the LORD; a footnote notes the Septuagint reads I am going to my home, matching verse 29. The claim to be bound for God's house sharpens the irony of his conduct that night.
The wicked men are literally sons of Belial, worthless and lawless men; the phrase marks them as utterly corrupt, the same kind of language used of the men of Sodom.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Judges 19:1-30, all 30 verses. Prose narrative. No em or en dashes present in the source or introduced; none anywhere in the file. This is a text of terror handled as description not prescription. Sermon set to none; no message from a trusted cluster was verified this session.
